content:
***A long time ago in a galaxy far, far away....***

# **STAR WARS**
## **THE GHOST IN THE RELAY**

*The **HIGH REPUBLIC** is a golden age of peace and ambition. Across the stars, the Chancellor’s "Great Works" bring unity to the frontier, guided by the noble Jedi Knights and the tireless innovation of a thousand systems.*

*Dedicated to this vision of progress, expert engineer **LIS GARAH** finds himself at the heart of the Republic’s grandest projects. A master of droids and a wizard of wires, Garah’s technical brilliance is matched only by his growing exhaustion with the suffocating bureaucracy that follows every victory.*

*A critical communication relay on the jungle moon of **ALEEN** has suddenly gone silent, threatening the reach of the majestic **STARLIGHT BEACON**. Dispatched to the surface with a specialized team, Garah must navigate a world of tangled growth and ancient machinery to restore the signal. But beneath the humid canopy, a strange and silent enigma awaits, prepared to challenge the logic of the Republic’s finest mind....*

thread.name: Prelude: Ghost in the Relay (Lis Garah)

content:
The ramp of the Republic Shuttle *Gilded Wing* lowers with a hiss of pressurized steam, immediately replaced by the suffocating embrace of Aleen's atmosphere. It is a world of high-pressure humidity and seismic instability, known for its vast subterranean networks and the delicate, often temperamental, relationship between the surface and the depths. Lis remembers the briefing: Sigma-9 is more than just a comms hub; it is a primary "Starlight" navigation node. Without it, the "Beacon's" light grows dim in this sector, making hyperspace travel a gamble of gravimetric shears.

Lis steps onto the spongy, moss-covered soil, his yellow jumpsuit already beginning to cling to his skin. The heat is thick enough to chew, smelling of ozone and rotting vegetation. Ahead, the Relay Station rises—a tapering spire of ivory-colored plastoid and gold-leaf trim that marks the height of High Republic aesthetics. Or it *was*. Now, it is draped in a shimmering, translucent emerald growth that pulses with a slow, rhythmic light. The moss seems to be breathing.

Vaneff steps off the ramp behind him, her boots clicking sharply on the metal before sinking into the Aleenian loam. She is a woman of sharp angles and even sharper schedules, her pristine white administrative tunic already sporting a damp patch at the collar. She doesn't look at the beauty of the jungle; she looks only at her datapad. **"‘Garah, the preliminary sweep for atmospheric toxins is within acceptable parameters. Log it under Section 4-Alpha of your arrival report. We are already behind the reporting schedule for the Coruscant hub, and Master Estala will expect the encryption arrays back online by the mid-day cycle. Form 12-B requires a signature for the outer hatch breach, so please, try not to damage the plating.’"**

To Lis's left, Voss checks the power cell on his carbine. The Republic Ranger is a stark contrast to Vaneff—his gear is worn, mud-flecked, and practical. He moves with a hunter's grace, his eyes constantly scanning the canopy where the massive, colorful Aleenian avians have gone eerily silent. **"‘Forget the forms for a second, Hali,’"** he mutters, his voice a low gravel. He gestures toward a deactivated security droid slumped against the station’s base. The moss has woven itself through the droid's joints and wrapped around its head like a parasitic crown. **"‘Look at that, Lis. That moss isn't just growing on the droid; it’s holding it upright. Clocked, stay on your wheels. I don't want you getting tangled in those vines.’"**

The little T3-series droid, , chirps a series of worried, low-frequency whistles. The droid's sensory dish spins rapidly, picking up a massive electromagnetic signature that seems to be vibrating through the ground itself, radiating from *within* the sealed station.

Thorne is already on his knees near the outer hatch, a specialized magnifying lens hovering over his eye. He is a man who seems to exist three inches to the left of reality, his fascination with the moss far outweighing any concern for safety. **"‘Remarkable,’"** he whispers, reaching out a gloved finger toward a pulsing vein of moss. **"‘It’s not just absorbing the station's heat. Look at the way the bioluminescence matches the reactor’s thrum. It’s mimicking the station’s nervous system. Lis, if you trip the security lockout too aggressively, you might induce a systemic shock in the organism. I need a core sample of the primary junction before you start cutting wires.’"**

*Lis Garah pays little attention to the others at first, activating his Graviton Boots... even before being warned that the moss may be dangerous, the engineer cares little to get his precious tools gunked up with plant matter. He doesn't seem to mind the sweat, his jumpsuit already worn and stained with years of use, though in spite of the oil stains and scratches the armoured parts are still up to safety standards.*
*He gives a sigh turning his head to the sharp Vaneff.* **"Reports are to be logged in situations where the engineer is free from risks and when it does not impair the continuation of the job at hand... technically speaking, our esteemed ranger has yet to declare the area to be safe... as such for the well being of everyone I will make the very responsible choice not to wast... I mean, spend time making a report"** *His tone of voice is matter of fact, droning one like he was reading a script... however an hint of gloating is clear in his voice: he really doesn't care to make any report, not now and not later.* **"The moss is pretty but I'm here to fix the comm relay, if you wish to collect some, Doctor, you can do so but the safety of the local ecosystem is frankly not part of my directives"** *He will adjust his Mechanical Interface Visor briefly, focusing onto the moss infested droid afterwards... not because he cares to investigate the moss itself but rather to see if he can detect the mechanical designs of the security droid in spite of the damage caused by the plant matter covering it.*

Vaneff’s face flushes a blotchy red at the mention of safety protocols. She opens her mouth to retort, likely to quote a subsection of the *Administrative Oversight Act*, but she catches Voss’s cold, steady gaze and thinks better of it. She snaps her datapad shut with a sharp *clack*. **"‘Fine, Garah. Section 8-Gamma of the Field Safety Act... I will mark the report as "Pending Safety Clearance." But do not think I won't notice if you're *choosing* to spend that time tinkering with your droid instead of the encryption array.’"**

Dr. Thorne doesn't even look up from his magnifying lens, though he tuts softly. **"‘Directives, directives. You engineers are so fond of your straight lines and binary logic. The "local ecosystem" is currently rewriting the station's architecture, Lis. Ignoring it is like ignoring a fire because you're only hired to fix the plumbing.’"**

Lis ignores the chatter, his focus narrowing as the lenses of his Mechanical Interface Visor slide into place with a series of precise, metallic clicks. The world shifts into a blueprint of thermal signatures and wire-frame overlays. He hones in on the slumped security droid. Through the thick, pulsing veins of emerald moss, the visor identifies the unit’s chassis: a standard *Sentinel-4* Security Unit, a mainstay of High Republic frontier stations. 

However, the diagnostic overlay is a mess of error codes. The moss isn't just covering the joints; it has forced its way into the maintenance access ports on the droid's neck and chest. Instead of standard lubricant or hydraulic fluid, the visor detects a bioluminescent sap flowing through the droid's internal conduits. The plant matter has effectively "high-jacked" the droid's motor-control subroutines.

Voss shifts his weight, his carbine’s targeting laser painting a red dot on the droid’s moss-choked head. **"‘Lis, keep that visor sharp. The thing's optics just cycled red for a fraction of a second. I think it’s trying to stand up, and I don't think it’s going to ask for our chain-codes.’"**

As Lis watches, the moss-covered Sentinel-4 gives a sudden, violent twitch. A wet, tearing sound echoes through the clearing as organic vines snap against the station's hull. The droid's single photoreceptor burns with a sickly, overcharged green light, and it begins to pull its chassis out of the "embrace" of the station wall.

*Instinctively, Lis puts his hand on his gun, a pristine heavy blaster with no wear... indeed, the engineer hardly ever needed the piece, it was mostly there in case of emergencies, backed up by standard training issues by any crew onboard vessels against potential raiders and pirates.*
**"...beyond reworking the wiring... the internals of the droid are filled with sap... son of a hutt!"** *He finally grabs the blaster proper, lifting it against the droid-plant aberration.* **"How in the FORCE is a plant taking over a droid?! It's working the hydraulics! Is this thing even an actual plant?!"** *Quickly, Lis Garah will bring up the Breath Mask dangling from his neck onto his mouth, fearing to get infected by this weird cybernetic parasite, clearly he looks panicked as the security droid seems to lift himself back from the dead.*

Lis’s thumb snaps the safety off his heavy blaster, the weapon gleaming in the harsh, humid Aleenian sun—a pristine relic of "just in case" that has suddenly become his most vital tool. The breath mask seals against his face with a sharp hiss of filtered oxygen, muffling his panicked breaths and replacing the scent of ozone with the sterile taste of recycled air. Through the polarized lenses of his visor, the world is a chaotic overlay of warning icons.

Voss doesn't flinch. He keeps his carbine leveled at the Sentinel’s chest, his finger hovering over the trigger. **"‘Steady, Garah. Don't start blasting until we know if it’s just the droid or if the whole station is going to react to the noise. Thorne, back up! Now!’"**

Thorne ignores the Ranger, his eyes wide behind his lenses. **"‘It’s beautiful! It’s not just working the hydraulics, Lis—it’s *optimizing* them. Look at the pressure levels! It’s as if the plant and the machine are forming a... a symbiotic intelligence. It’s not a parasite; it’s an upgrade!’"**

Vaneff, however, is already three steps back toward the shuttle. **"‘Upgrade? It’s a violation of Republic property! Garah, if that thing destroys the encryption array, the liability will be astronomical! Lock it down or shut it off!’"**

The Sentinel-4 fully detaches from the wall, trailing long, emerald strands of pulsing moss that look like torn tendons. It lets out a garbled, metallic screech that ends in a wet gurgle. It doesn't charge—not yet. It stands there, head tilting at an impossible angle, its glowing green photoreceptor scanning Lis with a disturbing, predatory focus.

The T3 droid whistles a frantic series of binary warnings. *Beep-boop-whirrr!* Clocked is highlighting the station door. Through your visor, you can see what the little droid is worried about: the moss isn't just on the surface. Thick, trunk-like vines have burrowed *into* the primary data-ports of the outer hatch. The station isn't just locked; its computer "brain" is being actively rewriten by the growth.
The hijacked droid's joints hiss with over-pressurized sap as it takes a heavy, clanking step toward the team.

*Lis continues to point the blaster at the accursed thing, still in a panic... before something clicks in his mind... the moss is a living being. The security droid's purpose is to defend. If the moss took over the droid and is assessing a potential threat is because of a lack of perceived authorisation.* **"Voss ready to shoot if it tries anything while I'm doing this... I'm sending my credentials to the droid... alongside some basic maintenance procedures and intel on what may have harmed the relay node..."** *Parasitic plant or not... this is a droid, with a digital mind, with parameters and programming... you may hack its perception of friend and foe, you may claim ownership and rewrite its directives... but the code that gives it autonomy is still the same... isn't it?* "**Let's hope this symbiotic 'intelligence' understands that I'm here to fix critical failures in the facility before it all goes to the jawas..."** *He continues sending credentials, blueprints, maintenance procedures... he even sends bureaucratic documents and the various safety measures necessary to ensure a safe healthy environment for life to thrive! By all accounts, Lis Garah is auditing the moss!*

Lis’s fingers fly across the interface of his wrist-mounted datalink, his breath mask hissing with every sharp, adrenaline-fueled inhale. He beams a frantic stream of data at the looming Sentinel-4: Republic engineering credentials, maintenance subroutines, and a massive dump of the very bureaucratic safety protocols he usually despises. Through his visor, he sees the data-transfer bar fill, the packets of information slamming into the droid's moss-clogged receiver.

The effect is immediate and strange. The hijacked droid freezes, one heavy leg mid-stride. The high-pressure hissing of the biological sap in its joints subsides into a low, rhythmic gurgle. The moss covering its chest plate pulses with a new cadence—no longer a predatory thrum, but a rapid, flickering emerald light that seems to mimic the scrolling data of the safety forms Lis is feeding it.

His deep understanding of biological systems reveals a startling truth: the moss isn't just a parasite. It is a highly sensitive bio-conductor that has integrated with the station’s distress signals. It *felt* the reactor redlining and interpreted the station's mechanical failure as environmental trauma. By sending it "Audit" data and stability protocols, Lis isn't just hacking a machine; he is providing the biological hive-mind with a *plan* to achieve the stability it craves. The organism is literally reading the Republic safety standards as a blueprint for survival.
Voss lowers his carbine an inch, though his finger remains tense on the guard. **"‘It stopped. I don’t know what you did, Garah, but the thing is just... standing there. It’s like it’s thinking.’"**

Thorne edges closer, his magnifying lens vibrating with excitement. **"‘It’s not thinking, Kaelen—it’s processing! Look at the veins in the moss! They’re shifting color to match the Republic’s data-transfer frequency.’"**

The T3 unit rolls forward and lets out a series of triumphant, rhythmic whistles. It bumps its chassis against the station's outer hatch, which suddenly emits a heavy, mechanical *thunk*. The moss vines burrowed into the door's seams retract just enough to allow the magnetic seals to disengage.
The heavy ivory-and-gold door groans as it begins to slide upward, revealing a dark corridor choked with thick, glowing emerald "cables" of moss that stretch deep into the station’s heart.

*The panic in the engineer slowly fades into nothing, the droid surrenders to the audit, the doors open to his credential... Lis Garah is in control once more, as such there's no need to fear: after sending a report on proper ventilation procedures for efficient removal of pollutants he even disengages the breath's mask vitals... though he keeps a close eye on the toxin detector worn as a ring, just in case.* **"Only doing my job..."** *Another data packet is sent to the droid... or maybe to the entire moss system? It includes laws, squatter's rights and a job application, to formalize the moss new's home and potential career within the High Republic.* **"... the moss may be a new alien life all things considered... Miss Vaneff, please... uh..."** *He waves his hand in a dismissive manner.* **"...do whatever it is expected for you to do in the situation of finding squatters taking care of Republic equipment... their potential recruitments... the finding of a new lifeform? I sent it a job application to so you may want to get ahead on that... heh..."** *He chuckles, as he holster his blaster, the gun going yet again unused.* **"... I'd like to see what kind of union an entire planet worth of grass is going to make..."** *He begins to walk forward, a bit tentatively, checking his visor to make sure the door's hydraulics won't suddenly give in either crush his body or seal him inside... if nothing dangerous was reported, he'd just shrug and walk in, ready to start his formal inspection of the facility... and, on second thought, he's quite glad he's the only person right now not stomping over the quasi-sentient cybernetic moss.*

The heavy blast doors remain open, the ivory-and-gold plating trembling slightly as the emerald moss thickens its grip on the hydraulic pistons. Through Lis’s visor, the mechanical diagnostic confirms a strange stability: the moss is actively "reinforcing" the door's weight-bearing struts with its own high-tensile fibers. It isn't just letting him in; it’s holding the path clear, responding to the stability protocols Lis beamed into its collective network. The toxin-sensing ring on his finger remains a steady, reassuring blue—whatever spores are in the air are currently filtered by the station's straining environmental scrubbers, or perhaps the moss is holding them back as part of its new "employment" agreement.

Vaneff stares at her datapad, her eyes blinking rapidly as she tries to process the data-packets Lis just sent. **"Squatter's rights? Unionization? Garah, the Republic Outreach Manual doesn't even have a *sub-paragraph* for collective bargaining with a bryophyte colony! If the Ministry of Commerce finds out I’ve "hired" a biological hazard, I’ll be demoted to a weather station on Hoth!"** Despite her sputtering, her fingers instinctively begin creating a new folder on her datapad labeled *Bio-Industrial Labor Anomalies*.

Thorne steps over a pulsing emerald vine with the grace of a man walking through a cathedral. **"Don't be so narrow-minded, Hali. Lis has done something fascinating. He’s assigned a purpose to a hive-intelligence that was previously in a state of environmental panic."** He looks at Lis with a new glimmer of respect. **"By formalizing its presence, you’ve stabilized the feedback loop. Just make sure the "labor contract" includes not squeezing the reactor to death."**

Voss remains at the rear, his carbine tucked into his shoulder as he watches the pacified Sentinel-4. The droid remains perfectly still, its single eye flickering in time with the station's emergency lights. **"Contract or not, I’m staying back here until the kid fixes the main breaker. Clocked, stay ahead of him. If the floor starts eating your treads, let us know."**

Lis leads the way into the corridor. Inside, the ivory walls are barely visible beneath a web of glowing green "nerves." The air is cooler here, smelling of damp stone and ionized oxygen. Through his visor, the engineer can see the primary power lines—usually buried behind wall panels—have been partially exposed. The moss has peeled back the durasteel plating to *hug* the cables, drawing heat and data. The thickest trunk of the organism weaves along the ceiling, leading like a massive, organic conduit toward the heavy circular hatch of the Reactor Core. 

A low, melodic chime echoes through the hall. It’s the station's internal clock, trying to signal the mid-day cycle, but the sound is distorted, coming out as a haunting, echoing woodwind note as the moss vibrates in sympathy with the frequency.

*Lis doesn't care much for the praise nor for the admonishments: this moss to him is no different than any other bum that decided to make a home under the glory of the High Republic's infrastructure... matter of fact, with the moss acting as some sort of cybernetic parasite, he just keeps sending reports to Aleen's communication relay, hoping that the moss will be able to receive the data sent and fix all the issues he sees.* **"Mhn... can you two keep quiet? ... some of the infrastructure was damaged... I need to make sure the locals have the right schematics so they don't harm the foundations of the facility... the blueprints are out of date so I have to send the right data manually..."** *True to his word, Garah was just just that, sending data regarding the exposed wires (alongside some binaric reprimands for letting wires be exposed in a humid environments) alongside warnings regarding potential structural weaknesses and even orders to check load bearing structures... he's just treating the moss like another droid!* **"Clocked, can you check if you can somehow connect to the communication system of the facility? If the moss could send us a status report it would make it easier for me to know what is the problem... would be pointless to go try and figure out what's wrong with the reactor when it may simply be a security measure locking the entire energy production to avoid potential damage... as far as I know, with all this moss, it could simply be the cooling system locking everything up...**"

As Lis’s reprimands and structural warnings flood the relay’s local network, the emerald moss reacts with a series of quick, rhythmic pulses. The thickest vines coiling around the exposed ceiling cables begin to constrict, secreting a heavy, translucent resin that hardens instantly into a makeshift insulation. The "locals" are indeed following the new schematics, tidying up the hazardous zones as if they were a crew of eager, if slightly confused, maintenance droids.

The little T3 droid chirps a series of sharp, affirmative whistles and rolls toward a wall-mounted diagnostic terminal. The screen is cracked and half-overgrown, but Clocked extends a scomp-link arm, pushing past a cluster of small, glowing spores. After a few seconds of frantic binary chirping and spinning his head-turret, a distorted holographic status report flickers into existence over the droid’s sensory dish.

The data is a chaotic mix of High Republic standard readouts and strange, undulating wave-patterns. Clocked projects a translation: The reactor’s cooling system hasn't failed; it has been intentionally throttled to 15% capacity to maintain a high-heat environment. 

The air grows significantly hotter as they stand before the hatch. The woodwind chime from earlier has become a steady, vibrating hum that Lis could feel if his boots were touching the ground.

*Lis rolls his eyes.* **"I was right... the moss has suppressed the cooling system... on... on PURPOSE?!"** *Immediately, Lis Garah goes on his Visual Wrist Comm, unloading a stream of data, angrily sending all the security protocols about the extreme dangers of messing with the cooling system of the reactor, the potential ecological disasters of a meltdown of other such high energy facilities, the potential destruction in case of a detonation and, worst of all: the destruction of the High Republic's most valuable assets! After the digital beatdown sent onto the moss, Lis runs his hand through his hair.* **"Thankfully the safety protocols kicked in... otherwise this entire facility would've been lost... ah well... can't blame a literal chunk of dirt from not realizing this..."** *He goes back to the comm, stopping the constant assault of warnings, of history disaster and safety procedures... instead, he begins sending to the moss a series of calibrations and instructions on how to utilized and program the cooling system properly, the right temperatures at which the reactor could work safely and other such practical information that could help an aspiring engineer figure out how this works... matter of fact, he will begin recording himself and sending the live recording to the moss system: he will show the plant first hand how to handle this.* **"...right I also have to explain it to the rest of you... so the moss has reduced the cooling system capacity to increase the heat surrounding the area... I assume from the scalding air the reactor did not explode and has either shut down recently, is constantly restarting itself or is running at an incredibly low capacity facing the extreme heat... I'd like to remain hopeful in saying the situation can be salvaged... oh, Clocked: run a binary translation of anything I'm saying, so that the plantlife may understand what I am doing... hopefully with the new intel I transmitted, the cooling system will start..."
**"... and if doesn't start at the proper capacity... uh... well it's going to be a real sweaty job for me... so I think we can afford to wait a few minutes...**"

The air in the antechamber shimmers with escalating heat, the once-cool ivory walls now radiating a feverish warmth that makes the emerald moss pulse in a frantic, overcharged rhythm. As Lis’s digital "beatdown" slams into the station's network, the reaction is visceral. The moss vines nearest the terminal shrivel and pull back as if scorched by the data itself, the warnings of catastrophic meltdown serving as a profound biological deterrent.

When the stream shifts to the instructional calibrations and the live recording of Lis’s own technical movements, the moss responds with terrifying efficiency. The thick, resinous webs covering the reactor hatch begin to liquefy and drain away into the floor gratings. The mechanical seals of the heavy circular door groan, then rotate with a sharp, pneumatic *hiss*.

The little droid’s head spins 360 degrees, its internal speaker emitting a rapid-fire sequence of binary pings that sound suspiciously like a teacher’s rhythmic tapping on a chalkboard. ***"‘[Translation: Sequence acknowledged. Thermal parameters accepted. Initiating compliance.]’"***
Thorne steps into the reactor room the moment the gap is wide enough. The heat inside is a physical wall, smelling of ozone and sweet, ripening fruit. **"‘Incredible... look at the core!’"**

Beyond the hatch, the Reactor Core is no longer a sterile piece of High Republic engineering. It has been transformed into a massive, glowing emerald "bulb." The reactor's cooling fins are draped in hundreds of translucent, flower-like pods that are drawing power directly from the radiation. However, as Lis’s stabilization frequency begins to broadcast through the room’s internal speakers—a low-frequency hum he calculated with surgical precision—the pods begin to change. They shift from a bloated, angry red to a calm, steady violet. 

A heavy, industrial thrum fills the room. It’s the sound of the cooling pumps finally forcing their way past the organic obstructions. The temperature on Lis’s visor begins a slow, steady descent. The moss isn't dying; it is *calibrating*. It is thinning its growth around the intake valves, following the blueprint Lis provided to ensure the incubator doesn't become a bomb.

*Lis nods as he sees the moss being as efficient as any other droid work force, his will manifested with the precision only years of studying and practice can give you... and with that same knowledge, he takes out more specialised tools, an actual heat sensor and a radiation detector.* **"Alright... let's see... everything seems to be working as intended..."** *He says, going through the hatch using his Graviton Boots to help him remain steady, as he tries to avoid the moss like one would avoid small working droids.* **"Give me some space to calibrate it... background radiation is... well... let's check in a bit if it actually lowering... the heat is improving... now... you could potentially place some of your network closer to the reactor... but you need to make sure the heat is not sticking close to it... maybe could use your fuzz as a heat shield of sorts..."** *He gently touches the moss with a hand, to get a feeling of how insulating the material actually is.* **"...I certainly can't improvise it on the spot as it stands... if you can send me some data regarding your own composition... maybe your insulation capabilities... your transpiration abilities... regardless this whole setup is just inefficient..."** *He puts his hands on his hips, as he looks at the moss, who foolishly attached itself to the reactor, still streaming his current 'audit' to the moss and the rest of the group if they care to tune their own comms to it.* **"The reactor ultimate function is to produce energy... heat... noise... movement... those are only side products... if you want more heat you could just redirect part of the reactor's energy to a different mechanism that has the sole purpose of making things hotter... honestly, if you were on the grid optimising the energy output you could have the entire facility running at top efficiency and have plenty leftover to power whatever incubation chamber you want..."** *He talks to the moss like he would talk to any other fellow worker.*

*As luck would have it, Lis Garah truly was the right man for the job today... after all who else could converse with a literal plant in such a way?*
*Indeed, he was even growing fond of this moss... not unlike droids... no emotions... no chatter... no appeals to some twisted sense of authority... just directives, clear purpose and straight forward needs.*

Lis glides across the floor, the magnetic hum of his graviton boots providing a steady anchor against the vibrating deck plates. He avoids the thickest patches of the moss with a practiced eye, treating the emerald growth with the same professional distance he’d give a cluster of high voltage capacitors or a team of low-level maintenance droids.

When Lis’s gloved hand makes contact with the moss to test its insulation, the texture is surprising—not slimy or brittle, but yielding and strangely dense, like heavy duty felted insulation. Beneath the surface, a rhythmic twitching suggests the plant is mimicking the mechanical pulse of the reactor pumps. In response to his technical lecture on thermal efficiency, the moss nearest his hand shudders. A fine mist of translucent sap beads on the leaves, and the "nerves" of the organism begin to glow with a steady, inquisitive violet.

*Everything seemed in order... well, as in order one could expect from a living nexus of plant matter who somehow understood how to hijack an entire communication infrastructure, reactor included.* **"Alright... these parameters are fuctional for the time being..."** *He slowly exited the hatch, returning to the previous area.* **"Clocked, can you check registry to see exactly what is the issue with the communication relay? I assume it was simply the new inhabitant's meddling with the reactor that may have shut down the operation... but it never hurts to control twice."** *He stretches, spending quite a few seconds losing up his muscles, as he stashes his calibrations tools for now and briefly disengages the visor, quite tired of the warning signs constantly reminding him of the moss infesting the various mechanisms.* **"I assume it may need a reboot... but on the telecommunication part I am only vaguely aware of the bus mainframes of the station itself: I am not too familiar with the setup that was installed here**" *Then he suddenly stops, turning to the rest of the team... he just realized that this entire time he actually ignore his fellow humans.* **"...oh and you guys can... uh... help too... however you want...?**"

The intense heat radiating from the reactor hatch has already subsided to a manageable warmth. Inside the core room, the emerald moss pulses with a gentle, steady violet light, the frantic rhythm of incubation replaced by the calm, efficient thrum of a system following its new programming. Lis steps back into the antechamber, the heavy circular hatch sealing behind him with a quiet *hiss*. He stretches, the motion cracking a satisfying series of pops from his spine as he holsters his unused blaster.

At Lis's command, Clocked swivels and rolls to a dusty, secondary terminal overgrown with thin, exploratory moss tendrils. The droid extends its scomp link, pushing the organic matter aside and plugging directly into the station's mainframe with a shower of blue sparks. After a moment of rapid-fire binary, a simplified log file appears on the droid's projected holodisplay.


The registry is clear: approximately seventy-two hours ago, the reactor's primary coolant regulator began to fluctuate wildly. As a standard safety precaution, the station's Master Control Program initiated a "Tier-3 Failsafe," systematically shutting down all non-essential, high-power systems to prevent a core breach. The long-range comms array was the first to go dark.

Vaneff leans over Clocked’s projector, her sharp eyes scanning the logs. The tension in her shoulders visibly eases. **"‘A failsafe. So the array isn't damaged, just dormant. Excellent. Garah, that means a simple system reboot from the primary comms mainframe should restore the connection. The mainframe is on the upper level, past the central hub.’"**


Lis's belated attempt to include the team hangs in the air for an awkward moment. Vaneff simply raises a single, unimpressed eyebrow before turning her attention back to her datapad, already drafting a preliminary success report.

Voss, however, just nods curtly and points his carbine down the dark, moss-choked corridor leading further into the station. **"‘Upper level means more ground to cover. And more of whatever that thing was,’"** he says, gesturing back toward the now-sealed reactor room. **"‘Stay sharp.’"**

**"Alright then"** *Lis Garah deactivates the magnetic grip of his boots and re-activates the magnetic floating, to continue not to harm the moss.* **"I'll probably need Clocked... Voss, you can come too if you want to give me cover but considering the situation I am hoping there will be no hostilities at hand"** *He taps the recording part of his wrist comm.* **"And if there is any issues, hopefully the moss will either help or warn me ahead of time... who knows maybe it will figure out a way to communicate directly with us by the time I'm done..."** *After he's done messing with the device he instead begins tapping his chin.* **"... I didn't think about it but I'm pretty much making first contact here...? Well... I think nobody else could've made a better impression! Alright, time to finish this"** *Considering the job appears to be a simple rebooting of a mainframe, Lis Garah doesn't even bother to ready his personal multitool, chances such a simple job could be handled by the droid if not by the moss itself.*
*As such, without much fear or expectation of hardships to come, he heads to the moss-chocked corridor.*

Lis floats forward, his graviton boots humming a quiet counter-melody to the station's distorted chimes. He glides effortlessly over the thick, emerald carpet, a silent engineer in a silent, living cathedral.

Voss nods, falling into step behind him, his heavy boots making no sound on the resilient moss. He keeps his carbine at a low ready, his gaze sweeping the overgrown corridor. **"‘Cover's my job, Lis. You just keep the lights on.’"**

The corridor opens into the station's central hub, a large, circular chamber where multiple levels and walkways converge. The space is a breathtaking spectacle of alien transformation. Massive, glowing vines stretch from the floor to the high ceiling, forming organic pillars. The moss has become an architect.
Clocked rolls ahead, its sensor dish spinning. Suddenly, the little droid stops and emits a sharp, cautionary beep, projecting a red warning icon onto the floor just ahead.

Lis’s keen eye catches the danger a split-second before the droid’s warning. The floor plate directly under a massive, pulsing nexus of moss sags almost imperceptibly. Hairline stress fractures, nearly invisible under the green glow, spiderweb out from the edges of the plastoid panel. The moss, in its blind hunger for the primary data conduit running beneath the floor, has torn a critical support strut clean from its housing. The entire section of walkway is balanced on a knife's edge.

*He engages the visor proper once more, looking around and determining the structural integrity of the area.* **"Clocked is right... this entire place seems a bit... messy structurally speaking... mhn..."** *He begins transmitting data to the tower with the intent of communicating in binary to the moss, specifically he seems to be sending the issues with the structural integrity of the walkway and requesting the moss reinforces it to make it safe to pass.* **"Let's see how diligent of a worker our fellow first contact alien is... requesting it to reinforce the way forward to make it safe for us to cross... well I have my boots so I could easily just scale the wall but you two... mhn... I really need to get that magnetic upgrade to you Clocked... anyway don't walk off Voss, wait until me or Clocked give you the greenlight"**

Lis’s visor snaps back into place, bathing his vision in a cool, analytical blue light. The wire-frame overlay highlights the compromised floor plate in a pulsing red, confirming the critical stress fractures. With a few deft commands on his wrist-link, he isolates the structural schematics and beams them directly into the station’s local network—a direct data-plea to the bio-intelligence currently holding the station hostage.

The response is immediate and profoundly alien. The massive nexus of moss above the weakened walkway begins to pulse with a rapid, intense violet light. Thick, sinewy vines descend from the ceiling, wrapping around the sagging floor plate with the deliberate precision of a master construction droid. They snake into the stress fractures, secreting a translucent, quick-hardening resin that glows with the same violet hue. The groaning of stressed plastoid is replaced by the quiet, wet sounds of organic reinforcement. Within seconds, the sagging floor is buttressed by a lattice of living, glowing buttresses. The path is stable.

Voss lets out a low whistle, his carbine held loosely at his side. **"‘Never seen a plant take orders before. It’s still not right... but it’s solid. I’ll give it that.’"** He takes a cautious step onto the newly reinforced section, testing it with his full weight. It doesn't budge. **"‘Alright, Garah. It's safe. Lead on.’"**

The way forward is clear. The central hub is a silent, breathtaking testament to Lis's strange, first-contact diplomacy. The path to the upper-level comms mainframe is now open, winding up a ramp that is also held in the moss's emerald embrace.

*Seeing the various colors flashing around, Lis Garah doesn't stop just there, sending another message to the moss hivemind, specifically the rough weight of himself, Clocked and Voss, going for excess.* **"Frankly with this type of efficiency, I'd like for this plant to become the staple in all of our stations and construction areas... depending on the resources needed to keep it alive, the Republic may very well face a new industrial revolution.. speaking of which..."** *After he's done sending the weight data, Lis Garah will then send an additional request: for the moss to change color in areas that cannot support such weight or areas that are either harmful for the trio or harmful for the moss to go through.* **"...I requested for the moss to give us visual cues, so we can avoid unsafe areas... really, maybe I should get a patch for myself and integrate it with some technology back home..."** *As he says this, he immediately regrets it... the fact that he voiced his opinion may be a liability in the future if he indeed decides to grab a sample for himself... he gives a sigh, as he realize because of this single sentence he's likely to be searched on station for any moss residue.*

As Lis finishes transmitting the complex data-stream, the entire chamber seems to hold its breath. For a moment, the ambient green glow of the moss dims, and the main lumin-panels overhead flicker violently, a clear sign of a massive power draw from the station's secondary systems. A low hum vibrates through the deck plating as a distant capacitor bank is drained to fuel this new directive.

Then, the chamber comes alive. The violet glow of the reinforced walkway fades, replaced by a new wave of energy that ripples out from the central moss nexus. A clean, pulsating line of pure white light appears on the floor, tracing a path directly from the team's position, across the stabilized walkway, and up the winding ramp toward the upper levels. Simultaneously, in a dark corner of the hub where the moss looks thin and withered around a sparking electrical junction, a section of the wall begins to pulse with a dull, cautionary red. The bio-intelligence has learned its new language.

*He smirks, as he sees the moss yet again being so useful and efficient.* **"Look at this Voss... isn't this amazing? Imagine an entire space station draped in this moss... the moment any raider or pirate goes aboard, you could have the plant warn you, tell you where the enemy is... maybe even warn you of breaches and other dangers... I can't wait to how this alien lifeform will integrate with our society!"** *While some could mistake his excitement with the fervor of Thorne, it is obviously not so: indeed assuming the downsides of the moss can be kept at bay, the potential of having a lifeform that integrates so seamlessly into both the architecture of a place and its digital infrastructure could be a huge leap forward in new engineering and technological advancements.* **"If this thing also produces oxygen it pretty much is set to have a great future within any Republic starship... oxygen production is such a hassle... I was basically FORCED to take Life Sciences courses to ensure I could work on the beacon..."** *As he moves forward, following the moss's guidance, he pats Clocked on the its metallic head.* **"And don't worry Clocked... you won't get obsolete... there's always room for upgrades!"**

Lis glides up the glowing white ramp, his excitement a palpable energy that seems to make the humid air crackle. The path leads them to the station's upper spire, where the moss is less a consuming entity and more a delicate, living embroidery tracing the architectural lines of the walls.

Voss keeps pace, his gaze constantly sweeping the junctions where the ceiling meets the walls. He listens to Lis’s enthusiastic speculation and grunts, his voice a low counterpoint to the engineer's fervor. **"‘Warns you of pirates, or maybe it *is* the pirate. Don't get too friendly with it, Lis. We still don't know what it wants.’"**
The little T3 droid beeps a cheerful, warbling tone in response to Lis's pat, its dome swiveling to keep its primary photoreceptor focused on its master.

The white path terminates at a heavy blast door, its designation stenciled in faded High Republic gold: **PRIMARY COMMUNICATIONS MAINFRAME**. The door hangs slightly ajar, a sliver of darkness visible within, from which the glowing path emanates. Voss gestures, and the team stacks up as he carefully pushes the door open.

The room beyond is the station's communication nerve center. The central mainframe is a massive cylindrical column of humming processors and blinking status lights, but it has been transformed. Thick, glowing emerald 'cables' of moss have grown directly into the mainframe's primary data conduits, bypassing the outer casing entirely. They pulse in perfect synchronicity with the machine's own operational rhythm. At the base of the column sits the main command terminal, its screen alive but displaying a constant, scrolling cascade of what looks like organic cellular structures mixed with raw binary code.

*Lis Garah shrugs.* **"Could be the pirates: it seems to be an intelligent creature after all... but ultimately it's hard to me to assume hostility when it has been so compliant... the alternative would be to request more soldiers and chemical engineers to purge the entire area... and even then, since the moss is so ingrained with ALL of the structure, we'd basically be dooming the whole communication array... without the moss we lose infrastructure that we CANNOT lose... with the moss we potentially gain an ally that will push programming and engineering far into the future..."** *As he spots the communication mainframe, he follows closely with Voss, before looking at the console.* **"Ultimately, the fault lies in the lack of maintenance, right now I'm just trying to play with the cards I'm dealt, you know? Honestly, with how run down everything is... I'm wondering if the moss is to blame for all this damage or if we should be grateful it kept things running as long as it has... alright let's see... Clocked give me a hand in figuring this out... careful for scrap data, I don't want you to glitch out"** *He will start to take control of the mainframe, while checking the screen... first of course he will give the console itself a quick visual checkup with his visor, ensuring that he isn't risking touching a live wire or some other obvious hazard, then he will tentatively start with some basic console commands, to see how the plant machine hybrid responds to his attentive care.*

Lis’s visor slides back down, overlaying the world with diagnostic data. The terminal itself is physically safe; the moss has carefully woven its tendrils around the primary power junctions, drawing energy without causing a short. The energy readings, however, are a chaotic mess—two distinct systems, one biological and one mechanical, are fighting for dominance within the same circuits.

Ignoring the unstable readings, Lis places his hands on the console. The surface is unexpectedly warm and feels slick with a thin layer of condensation. He begins typing, inputting a standard Republic diagnostic command—a gentle query to check the system’s core OS.

The result is instantaneous and jarring. The terminal screen flashes from a scrolling green-and-black cascade to a solid, screaming crimson. Every light on the cylindrical mainframe begins to blink erratically, and the thick moss cables wrapped around it shift from a calm emerald to an agitated, angry red. A low, dissonant hum fills the room, a sound of grinding gears and a pained biological groan all at once. The reboot sequence hasn't just failed; it has been rejected as a hostile command.

The little T3 droid emits a series of panicked, high-pitched whistles and projects a stark warning message in blinking red letters above the console: `[SYSTEM CONFLICT: ORGANIC/SYNTHETIC PROTOCOL INCOMPATIBILITY]`.
Voss, who had been watching the corridor, spins around, leveling his carbine at the glowing red mainframe. **"‘Lis! What in the blazes did you just do? It looks like you made it angry!’"**

*The sudden of gears grinding and biological pain doesn't really affect him too much emotionally... he instead thinks carefully about the next step... after all, machines cannot feel pain and the moss... well, whatever it feels it will have to deal with right now.* **"Wrong approach... alright my mistake... so far I handled this indirectly... maybe I should go the path of least resistance..."** *Instead of touching the console again, Lis goes back to his Wrist Comm, starting to quickly and (hopefully) efficiently stream more data to the moss: the data will explain the command used, its effects, why it must be enacted and how the programming to enact it works... basically, instead of the engineer trying to take over and control the situation, he will keep letting the moss figure it out and handle it itself.* **"I should probably send some... uh... apology... technically speaking this is the plant's body... it's a bit as if I tried to reprogram its... I don't know ears? Mouth?"** *After he sent the data stream, he tries to contact Vaneff with his commlink.* **"So I don't know if you can see it or if you can receive me... but I may have sliiiightly hurt the matrix of the moss... you're better at me at handling this, could you like.... I don't know, broadcast to the local system whatever we're supposed to do when we hurt the local life? Offer it food? Water? I don't know... what's the standard payment in case of wrongful dismemberment and/or manslaughter?"**

Lis works his wrist comm, his expression a mask of intense concentration. A new stream of data flows from his device into the station's network—a technical olive branch. The data packet isn't a command, but a lesson: a detailed explanation of the reboot protocol, complete with annotations and safety warnings, designed to teach the mycelial intelligence *why* the reboot is necessary for long-term stability.

The effect is not what he expects. The angry red glow of the mainframe dims to a confused, muddy purple. The dissonant hum shifts into a high-pitched, painful whine of stressed machinery. The thick moss cables wrapped around the central column constrict violently, squeezing the durasteel casing with immense biological force. A shower of yellow sparks erupts from a compromised junction box, and the main lights throughout the upper level flicker and die, plunging the entire area into a disorienting twilight lit only by the mainframe’s chaotic glow and the faint white path on the floor. The translation patch has failed; the moss is now trying to physically "crush" the conflicting synthetic command out of the system.

Voss takes a step back, his carbine now aimed squarely at the sparking junction box on the mainframe. He plants his feet, his form a dark silhouette against the glowing chaos. **"‘Lis, whatever you're doing, it's making things worse! You have about ten seconds before I solve this problem with high-energy particles!’"**

*Garah stops for a second, frustrated at the constant stream of errors he's facing as he tries to remotely direct the moss, he then barks back at Voss.* **"Oh I'm sorry did the MECHANICAL ENGINEER do a bad job at programming a SENTIENT/DROID HYBRID?!"** *He lets go of the wrist comm, deciding to try a more brutal approach... indeed, he's a mechanical engineer, so that's what he will try to handle: arming himself with his personal multitool, he tries to fix the issues at the base: if the mesh of moss and machine is the issue to his commands at this time, he will try to re-route control away from the moss, freeing the machine of any interference and removing non-critical components.* **"An ENTIRE building neglected for possibly decades overrun by a never seen before sentient entity... yeah of course we just send ONE guy to fix everything... of course if everything goes to the banthas I'll be the scapegoat... that's one way to end my bastard career!"** *He swears and rants to himself while gritting his teeth, rewiring the console on the spot so it will just execute the command without the moss trying to understand it.* **"Clocker, broadcast to the plant to just let the machines do their job! It doesn't need to do ANYTHING but let the already set programming and mechanisms operate!"** *He commands to the droid, as sparks fly way too close to his mouth, live wires threatening to bite at him as much as the moss.*

Sparks fly as Lis, abandoning all attempts at diplomacy, shoves his multitool into the console's access panel. He mutters curses under his breath, his knuckles scraping against fried circuits as he yanks a bundle of moss-choked wires free. The air fills with the acrid smell of burnt sugar and ozone. His goal is no longer to teach, but to sever. To perform emergency surgery on the machine and cut the biological infection out of the command loop.

The little droid, sensing his master's intent, rolls forward. It bypasses the console and interfaces with a nearby conduit that the moss has claimed. It begins broadcasting a simple, repetitive binary sequence; a stream of pure, calming logic. It's the digital equivalent of a steady, rhythmic hum, a distraction designed to draw the mycelial network's chaotic attention away from the surgical violence Lis is performing on its central nerve cluster.

The gambit works. For a critical second, the moss's violent constriction lessens. In that moment, Lis finds the primary command relay. With a final, forceful twist of his tool, he reroutes the connection, creating a direct hardware bridge that bypasses the organic growth entirely.

The angry red glow of the mainframe instantly cuts out. The painful, grinding hum ceases. The entire column goes dark for a single, silent beat before its processors hum back to life with a clean, synthetic tone. On the main terminal, the chaotic organic patterns are gone, replaced by a familiar sight: a boot sequence progress bar. A soft chime echoes through the room as the long-range comms array begins its power-up cycle.

Then, the station goes dark. The glowing white path on the floor vanishes. The ambient emerald light from the moss on the walls extinguishes completely. The only light comes from the terminal screen and the single beam of Voss's weapon-light. A deep, profound silence falls over the station, a silence that feels heavier and more menacing than the noise it replaced.

Voss sweeps his light across the now-darkened moss, then back to the corridor they came from. His voice is a low, tense whisper. **"‘Lis... the path is gone. Did you hear that?’"** From the direction of the Central Hub, a new sound echoes down the corridor: the heavy, rhythmic *clank... clank... clank* of a droid on the move.

*He takes a deep breath as he can finally work, the connection re-routed, he begins to relax ever so slightly, his brow salty with cold sweat.* **"Yes I think this was an important node for the moss... so I expect whatever droids were infected by it are not... well pissed... so let's see..."** *As Lis Garah continues handling the console, he can try to start making some proper modifications... he doesn't have much hope to succeed but killing an entire new lifeform that is so attuned to technology just doesn't sit right with him.* **"Give me cover..."** *He says, with a groan, as he tries to figure out something quick... he tries to apply the knowledge he has of his Life Sciences... after all he had to work often in making ecosystems for stations to make at least some of the air regenerates, with different species needing different plants, some plants being toxic to some and all that headache that comes with it... moss alongside lichen and other small plants sometimes played niche rolls in both adorning a station with greenery and also adding some oxygen output here and there and with huge spacestations or capital ships, a single nook dedicated to plant life is several million liters of oxygen one does not have to store in heavy and bulky... he tries to recall any information that may help reconnecting the moss to the central nerve while also ensuring it won't directly interfere with the mainframe, rather he's trying to allow the moss to use the mainframe's spare processing power in a separate instance from the mainframe itself, so that the machinery may follow its directives and the moss... well, may do whatever it needs to do to be alive.* **"What a mess... what a mess... this entire thing is a lose-lose situation for me..."**

Lis’s ears pick up the sound with practiced clarity. The clanking is heavy, methodical, and metallic—not the shuffling of a maintenance droid. It’s coming up the ramp they just ascended. The sound stops abruptly, just outside the darkened doorway. A profound silence follows, broken only by the hum of the rebooting mainframe. Then, a single, crimson photoreceptor cuts through the gloom, its beam sweeping methodically across the threshold of the room. It’s hunting.

Voss crouches low, flattening himself against the wall beside the doorway. He whispers, his voice barely audible over the hum of the console. **"‘Contact. Single unit, heavy chassis. It's waiting just outside. Lis, time's up.’"**

But Lis is already a blur of motion, his previous frustration replaced by a surge of desperate ingenuity. He ignores the threat at the door, his entire world narrowing to the guts of the mainframe. His multitool moves with a surgeon's precision, popping open a secondary panel to reveal a bank of auxiliary processing cores—redundant systems designed for diagnostic purges, now repurposed in a stroke of genius. He reroutes a secondary power conduit, physically isolating the cores from the main OS. It’s a masterful piece of hardware modification, creating a digital quarantine zone—a sandbox—in mere seconds.

The T3 unit, sensing its master's design, plugs its scomp link into the new connection, helping Lis flash-compile a new, simplified operating system onto the isolated cores. The code is elegant, a simple loop offering the raw power and data the moss craves, but walled off from the station's critical functions. It is, however, a purely mechanical solution, designed for a program, not an ecosystem. It offers unlimited food, with no consideration for unchecked growth.

As Lis makes the final connection, the Sentinel-4 droid steps through the doorway. The emerald moss that once covered it is now a dry, dead brown, flaking off its chassis like old paint. Its photoreceptor burns with a hostile crimson light, its weapon systems whirring as it acquires its targets.

At the exact same moment, the severed moss cables wrapped around the mainframe twitch. A single, tentative green tendril snakes out from the deadened mass and touches the port for the new, isolated system Lis just created. The terminal screen, still displaying the reboot progress bar, is suddenly flooded with a new window—a cascade of vibrant green code as the moss accepts its new home.

**"Alright Voss good news and bad news..."** *Garah swipes the sweat from his brow, the realization of what's happening hitting him like a bag of bricks as adrenaline is drained from his body, as he takes a couple valuable seconds to assess the situation.* **"Good news: I did not just genocide an alien species..."** *He quickly gets away from the mainframe, arming himself with the heavy blaster.* **"Bad news: I believe the moss is no longer in control of the droid and the last programming it may have received is to eliminate us..."** *Finally, his weary bruised fingers find a solid grip for the blaster, holding it steady and aiming it at the heavy security droid.* **"Let's hope the doctor and Vaneff didn't get blasted... not sure if the distress signal is powerful enough to reach out to anyone else..."** *With his other hand, he presses a small concealed button within an unassuming cheap looking metal ring... but that was in truth the engineer's lifeline: as the panic ring activates the distress signal is send everywhere, desperately broadcasting a message requesting support to any and all of the Republic that could possibly listen.* **"The floor is yours, Ranger"**

The moment the Sentinel-4’s crimson photoreceptor sweeps into the room, Voss is already moving. His readied carbine barks once, the bolt slamming into the droid's chest. Sparks fly as its personal energy shield gutters and dies, the shot scoring a deep, glowing gouge in the metal beneath.

The droid seems utterly unfazed by the impact. Its chassis pivots with cold, mechanical speed, locking onto the Ranger. Its integrated blaster rifle cycles with a terrifying *whine* and unleashes a single, perfectly aimed crimson bolt. The shot punches through Voss's defenses with devastating force, his armor hissing and smoking where the energy seared through. He grunts sharply, staggering but holding his ground, the smell of burnt plastoid sharp in the air.

Lis, seeing Voss take the brutal hit, raises his own pristine heavy blaster. He aims, squeezes the trigger, but is met with a dead, metallic *click*. The power cell indicator flashes an angry red—a critical misfire. The weapon is jammed, the shot never leaving the barrel, a testament to its lack of use.

Voss grits his teeth against the pain, his breathing ragged. He takes a moment to steady himself, drawing on his training to push past the agony. He raises his carbine again and fires, but the return shot goes wide, streaking past the droid's head to impact the far wall in a shower of sparks.

The Sentinel presses its advantage. A second integrated rifle unfolds from its other shoulder, and it unleashes a volley. One bolt slams into Voss, forcing a cry of pain from the Ranger as he stumbles back against a console. The second rifle, however, sputters violently. A plume of black smoke vents from its side as the weapon jams, its charging coils falling silent.

Frustration warring with panic, Lis quickly works his blaster, his engineer's fingers deftly ejecting the faulty power pack and slamming a new one home. He brings the weapon back up and fires a hurried shot, but the bolt sails harmlessly over the droid's shoulder, impacting a humming console in a spray of sparks.

*Lis continues to try and shoot the droid, alas while he many a time modified or even build a few blasters he was not an expert at shooting them, only occasionally doing brief recreative hunts while working on planets that supported creatures with tasty meat or valuable furs... but an actual faulty security droid targetting him like this?! Voss was pretty much on his own for this one... or he could always pray for the Force to let Garah somehow hit the droid.*

A ragged breath escapes Voss’s lips. He pushes himself off the console, his movements stiff and pained as he fights to clear his head. Ignoring the smoking ruin of his own armor, he brings the carbine up with a steady hand, the weapon's sights settling on the Sentinel’s central chassis. He fires. The bolt screams across the darkened room and slams into the droid with a percussive *thump*. The last vestiges of its energy shield vanish in a flicker of blue light, and the shot punches through metal, leaving a glowing, molten crater in the droid’s torso.

The droid staggers back a step, its crimson eye flickering for a moment. It completely ignores its jammed secondary weapon, its programming focused with single-minded lethality on the man who is still shooting. Its primary rifle whines back to full charge. Another bolt of crimson energy lances out, striking Voss in the chest. The Ranger is thrown back against the humming mainframe, a choked cry escaping his lips as he slumps to one knee, his carbine clattering against the deck. He is still conscious, but just barely.

Smoke pours from the seared plates of Voss’s armor. He’s on one knee, his breath coming in ragged, painful gasps. He glances over at Lis, seeing the engineer's wild, ineffective shots sparking against the far wall. A grim resolve settles in the Ranger's eyes. This is his job. This is what the Republic pays him for.

With a deep, guttural groan, he forces himself to his feet. A surge of pure, defiant will floods his system, a final reserve of strength that pushes back the encroaching darkness. He shoves himself away from the mainframe, his boots scraping against the deck as he deliberately moves away from the doorway, making himself the larger, more immediate threat. **"‘Hey, tin-can!’"** he snarls, his voice a raw bark of pain and challenge. **"‘You want me? Come and get me!’"**

The Sentinel-4 pivots, its crimson eye locking onto him. As its remaining weapon begins to whine, Voss brings his carbine up one last time. For a single, perfect moment, the universe narrows to the space between his sights and the droid’s photoreceptor. There is no pain. There is no fear. There is only the shot.

He fires.

The bolt of energy doesn't just strike the droid; it *lances* through it. The shot hits the crimson eye dead center, punches clean through the chassis, and detonates the droid's internal power core in a violent, blinding flash of white light. The Sentinel-4 is vaporized from the inside out. A wave of concussive force and molten shrapnel rips through the room, and the droid's final, agonized screech of tortured metal is cut short as its legs collapse and the smoldering ruin of its torso crashes to the floor.

Silence.

The strength leaves Voss in a rush. He slumps against the now-silent mainframe, his carbine held loosely in his hands. He looks at Lis, a grim, exhausted satisfaction in his eyes. **"‘My job... is to give you cover. Now... finish yours.’"**

*It takes a few seconds for Garah to calm down, looking at the droid making sure it is disabled.* **"...brilliant! Good job Ranger!"** *He smiles, another job well done... maybe the gravity not hitting him quite right or maybe he just doesn't realize the damage Voss has sustained.* **"This should be easier now..."** *First, he goes to check the job he made for the moss: he doesn't care about much else but making sure the moss and the mainframe aren't directly linked, so he won't have to repeat the experience... after that's confirmed, he'll go back to try and restart the communication array.* **"I will really have to request someone to keep checking this station... this disrepair and lack of maintenance is truly abhorrent... and they should've sent someone else with us! At minimum another engineer and another ranger! Tsk tsk tsk..."** *He clicks his tongue, as he continues his work.* **"Clocked, try to help me tune things up here... I foresee with all the damage and reprogramming things won't be that clear cut..."**

The acrid smell of a detonated power core hangs heavy in the air. Lis gives the smoldering ruin of the Sentinel-4 a wide berth, his focus already snapping back to the task at hand. His earlier panic is gone, replaced by the cool, confident hum of a professional in his element.

He glides back to the mainframe, his visor giving the console a final, satisfying diagnostic sweep. His partition is a thing of beauty—a clean, perfect hardware quarantine. The moss, now a vibrant, pulsing green, is happily siphoning power from the auxiliary cores, its tendrils dancing across the data Lis has provided, completely oblivious to the main system. The machine is a machine again.

At Lis’s command, Clocked rolls up to the main terminal, its scomp link plugging in with a decisive *click*. The little droid works in perfect sync with its master, its internal processors handling the minutiae of the boot sequence while Lis orchestrates the grand design from the primary console.

The reboot is not just successful; it's an act of technical artistry. With Clocked handling the sub-routines, Lis doesn't just restart the system—he optimizes it on the fly, writing new code to bypass damaged circuits and re-route power with breathtaking efficiency. The terminal screen, once a cascade of errors, now displays a clean, crisp High Republic interface. A final, satisfying chime echoes through the room as the screen declares: `LONG-RANGE COMMS ARRAY: ONLINE. SIGNAL STRENGTH: 92%`.

With a final hum, the main lumin-panels overhead flicker to life, flooding the room with clean, white light and banishing the last of the shadows. The job is done.

The sudden light makes Voss flinch. He's still slumped against the mainframe, his breathing shallow. The pristine light reveals the brutal reality of his injuries—a piece of shrapnel is embedded in his shoulder pauldron, and the front of his armor is a blackened, molten ruin. He looks over at Lis, a flicker of pained amusement in his eyes at the engineer’s oblivious chatter. He tries to push himself up, but his arm gives way, and he lets out a low, involuntary groan.

Vaneff's sharp, tinny voice cuts through Lis's internal commlink, the connection now crystal clear. ***"‘Garah, my datapad is registering a full signal lock with the Beacon. Confirm mission status. I am also logging critical bio-sign warnings for Ranger Voss. What is his condition? Report.’"***

*He gives a casual whistle as he fixes everything and a final smug tap to the console finalizes the setup.* **"There we go! That's a job well done if I do say so myself! A nice and crisp..."** *As the voice of Vaneff comes through the commlink, Garah begins walking towards Voss.* **"Yes yes... job is fine... and Voss is also very much OH CRAP!"** *He finally sees the mess that is the ranger's armor and how he's been blasted (almost) to high heaven.* **"WHY DIDN'T YOU SAY ANYTHING?!"** *The engineer exlaims, in a bit of a panic, his focus on the job having distracted him from the injuries Kaelen had suffered.* **"Uh... do you know how to use a medkit? I have compact one..."** *Then he turns back to the commlink.* **"Oh right Vaneff... aaaah... long story short, I re-activated communications... I genocided a new lifeform... temporarily... and then we got shot at by a security droid... well... Voss got shot by a security droid..."** *He looks at the ranger to confirm.* **"... multiple times... he got shot multiple times..."**

The pristine white light of the restored lumin-panels glints off the fresh scorch marks on Voss's armor. The Ranger manages a weak, grim smile, the expression pulling at the soot-stained skin on his face. He waves a dismissive hand at Lis’s panicked offer of the medpac. **"‘Was... busy,’"** he rasps, his voice strained. **"‘You were busy. Going to need... a proper medic for this one, Lis.’"** He tries to push himself to his feet, but his legs tremble and he slumps back against the mainframe, the effort leaving him pale and slick with sweat.

A beat of static crackles over Lis's commlink before Vaneff's voice cuts through, sharp and devoid of any of Lis's chaotic energy. ***"‘Genocide is not a reportable term in the Republic Field Manual, Garah. I am logging one (1) hostile robotic entity, neutralized. Status of Ranger Voss: Is he ambulatory? I am authorizing the use of the emergency trauma kit from the shuttle. Garah, secure your work and prepare for immediate evac. We are done here.’"***

*He gives a look over to Voss...* **"It's... pretty damn bad?"** *He goes to try and help him up, while Lis wasn't a Gamorrean he was used to lifting heavy machinery and tightening rusted bolts, chances were he could at least give enough support so Voss could handle a walk.* **"Let's get you back pal..."** *As he helps Voss up, he looks at the console... and shrugs.* **"Say Vaneff... I activated my panic ring... is extra security being dispatched?"** *He asks, as he continues to support the ranger, bringing him towards the spaceship.* **"Also... my work isn't secured at all nor it is going to: this entire facility is BEYOND scrapped! It will need at least a few standard months of maintenance, rewiring and integration... the moss almost fried the entirety of the system!"**

Lis glides over to the injured Ranger, the hum of his graviton boots a steady, quiet sound in the otherwise silent room. He has to dip low, his body tilting at an awkward angle to loop an arm under Voss's shoulder and take his weight.

The moment the fully armored Ranger leans on him, the boots protest. The quiet hum escalates to a strained, high-pitched whine. The repulsors sputter, and Lis wobbles precariously in the air, forced to brace himself with his core muscles just to maintain the half-meter altitude. It's less a supportive carry and more a controlled, floating stumble.

A sharp, involuntary grunt escapes Voss’s lips at the jarring motion. He leans heavily on the engineer, his own heavy boots scraping uselessly against the deck plating as he struggles to find purchase while being supported by an unstable, floating anchor. **"‘Thanks, kid…’"** he rasps, each word a visible effort. **"‘Just... get us to the shuttle.’"**

Vaneff’s voice crackles over the comm, sharp with administrative confusion. ***"‘Panic ring? Garah, there is no such device listed in the standard Republic field kit. What is its requisition form number? And what do you mean your work isn’t secured? The signal is stable. File the mission as complete per Protocol 7-Gamma.’"*** There’s a pause, followed by an exasperated sigh that carries clearly over the channel. ***"‘Just get the Ranger back here. Now.’"***

The journey back through the Central Hub is a slow, agonizing shuffle. Lis floats, wobbles, and strains, while Voss limps and scrapes beside him. As they cross the reinforced walkway, the station groans—a deep, resonant sound of stressed metal from somewhere far below. The main lumin-panels flicker once, threatening to plunge them back into darkness before stabilizing.

*In retrospect... the should've turned the boots off... but as he held Voss now, it was a bit too late, instead he tries to let the boots do most of the work and act as an improv suspension, allowing him to support Voss while at the same time letting not forcing any bump in the road to hurt him.* **"No problem... just... one second..."** *As Vaneff continues to speak, he holds the ranger steady, so he can reach his own commlink and shut it off.* **"Clocked, refuse further hailing from Miss Vaneff..."** *He then gives a heavy sigh, as he continue to carry the wounded man towards the ship.* **"That woman is ffffflipping useless..."** *He hisses... then he nods his head towards the droid.* **"Check the area ahead of us, let's make our way back lacking in mistakes, right?"**

The high-pitched whine of Lis’s graviton boots becomes a constant, grating sound in the corridor. Each step is a controlled lurch as he fights to keep both himself and the semi-conscious Ranger airborne. The boots were designed for a single operator, not for carrying a wounded man in full armor through a humid jungle station. The commlink on Lis’s wrist goes silent, cutting off Vaneff's tirade mid-sentence. The only sounds now are the straining boots, Voss’s ragged breathing, and the unsettlingly quiet hum of the station.

As they shuffle through the Central Hub, another deep groan echoes from below—louder this time, followed by the distant, echoing crash of collapsing metal. The reinforced walkway they stand on remains solid, but the station is clearly protesting the reallocation of its structural integrity.

Voss’s entire weight sags against Lis, his head lolling for a moment before he forces it back up, his jaw clenched. He notices the sudden silence from the comm and gives a faint, almost imperceptible nod. **"‘Good… call,’"** he gasps.

The T3 unit emits a sharp, two-tone chirp of acknowledgement. Its dome swivels, and it rolls ahead with renewed purpose, its single forward illuminator cutting a clean, white beam down the glowing path, scouting for any further structural anomalies on the route back to the shuttle.

*He continues to carry Voss over, with the droid ensuring they won't walk over a dangerous area... well dangerous for Voss: if Lis fell, he would likely just strain his knees.*
*He doesn't say anything... a few times he opens his mouth... looks around... then closes it... not really a charismatic fellow.*
*Not knowing what else to do, Garah used the 'ol reiable: a fake yawn, to express how tired he was and how long this day without actually having to say anything.*

The fake yawn is a dry, awkward sound in the humid corridor. It does little to break the tension. The only response is a weary shift of weight from the Ranger leaning heavily on Lis's straining frame.

The rest of the journey back to the shuttle is a slow, grueling test of the graviton boots' power cells and Lis's own endurance. They finally emerge from the station's dark maw into the oppressive heat of the Aleenian day. The clean, white ramp of the *Gilded Wing* waits like a gateway to a saner world.

Vaneff stands at the base of the ramp, her arms crossed, her datapad clutched like a weapon. Her expression is a thundercloud of administrative fury. Thorne is beside her, gently placing a sealed specimen container holding a single, pulsing emerald leaf into a cryo-chest.

As Lis and the limping Voss reach the shuttle, Vaneff points a sharp finger at Lis. **"‘Disengaging your commlink during a post-conflict stabilization is a Level-Two infraction, Garah! And I will be filing a formal complaint about your—’"** Her tirade cuts short as Voss stumbles, his full weight nearly taking Lis down. Vaneff’s eyes widen at the sight of the molten armor and the dark, spreading stain on the Ranger’s undersuit. **"‘Spirits… Get him to the medbay. Now.’"**

The flight back to the Starlight Beacon is a blur of humming engines and the sterile scent of the medbay’s bacta processors. Voss survives, stabilized by the shuttle's auto-doc and Lis's surprisingly steady hands during the initial patch-up. Vaneff files her report—a masterpiece of bureaucratic language that praises Voss for his "valorous adherence to engagement protocols" and notes Garah's "unorthodox but effective resolution," with a lengthy addendum on commlink etiquette. Dr. Thorne's specimen is whisked away to a high-security Republic xenobiology lab, never to be heard from by them again.

Lis receives a formal reprimand for his methods... and a case of Corellian ale left anonymously at his workshop door, a silent "thank you" from the Ranger. For a few weeks, the engineer finds himself the subject of quiet admiration among the Beacon's maintenance crews—the guy who "negotiated" with a parasitic plant and won.

He spends his time doing what he loves most: tinkering. In the quiet hum of his workshop, surrounded by the familiar comfort of machinery, he finally gets to work on the magnetic upgrade for Clocked, fulfilling his promise. For now, the galaxy is a series of fascinating problems to be solved, and he is, as always, the right man for the job.
**The End**
